terms.definition: A reusable algorithmic template (sliding window, BFS, DP, etc.) that fits a recurring shape of problem.
The skill of mapping a new problem to one (or two) known patterns within the first minute, often by matching shape, constraints and required output.
Recursive 'try, recurse, undo' search strategy for problems where you must enumerate / validate combinations or permutations.
Disjoint-Set Union structure with O(α(n)) amortized find/union; the go-to pattern for dynamic connectivity and MST.
Linear ordering of a DAG so every edge points from earlier to later; used for scheduling tasks with dependencies.
O(n) preprocessing of cumulative sums enabling O(1) range-sum queries; the foundation of many sub-array algorithms.
A stack maintained in non-decreasing or non-increasing order, enabling O(n) computation of next-greater / next-smaller elements.
Dynamic programming where the state encodes a subset of items as a bitmask; effective when n ≤ ~20.
